beats.prompt: Order statistics: ranking draws to find extremes.

interaction.title: Order Statistics
interaction.body:
**Order statistics** rank n IID draws from smallest to largest. The **k-th order statistic** U_(k) is the k-th smallest of n draws. For n IID Uniform(0,1):

- **E[max] = E[U_(n)] = n/(n+1)** — the largest draw sits above center, climbing toward 1 as n grows.
- **E[min] = E[U_(1)] = 1/(n+1)** — the smallest sits below 1/n, sinking toward 0.

Together they always sum to 1: E[max] + E[min] = n/(n+1) + 1/(n+1) = 1.

feedback.correct: Order statistics rank ordered draws — the max is the n-th, the min is the 1st.
hints: The k-th order statistic U_(k) is the k-th smallest of n IID draws.
E[U_(n)] = n/(n+1) — the max draw sits above center and climbs toward 1.
Tap Continue to move on.

beats.prompt: You've seen these before. Match each counting idea to its formula.

pairs.left: arrange n distinct items in order
ordered selection of k items from n

feedback.correct: n! counts all orderings; nPk ordered-picks k of n. Both count ordered structure — the doorway to order statistics.
hints: n! is every way to arrange n distinct items.
nPk orders k items chosen from n: n × (n−1) × … × (n−k+1).
n! matches 'arrange n', nPk matches 'ordered selection of k'.

beats.prompt: 500 ants stand at random spots on a 1-foot stick. Each marches at 1 ft/min; when two meet head-on they instantly reverse. How long until every ant has fallen off?

About 1 minute.note: Good instinct — let's prove it: relabeling collisions as pass-throughs turns this into the max of 500 positions.

Many minutes.note: Let's test it — collisions look slow, but relabeling erases them; the last ant just walks straight off.

Hours / intractable.note: Let's test it — the chaos is an illusion; swapping labels reveals a barely-over-a-minute answer.

beats.prompt: Two IID Uniform(0,1) draws. What is the expected value of the larger? The smaller?

feedback.correct: E[max]=2/3 and E[min]=1/3 — symmetric around 1/2, summing to 1. The larger draw sits above center.
hints: The larger of two draws beats the average. E[max]=n/(n+1)=2/3, and E[min]=1/3.
Plug n=2 into E[max]=n/(n+1).

beats.prompt: Watch 500 ants march and bounce. Toggle 'Swap labels on collision' — does it change when ants fall off?

hero.structuralReadout: With 500 ants, the last one off falls at 500/501 ≈ 0.998 min — barely over a minute.

feedback.correct: The last-off ant is the one that started farthest from its exit — the maximum of 500 starting positions.
hints: The set of fall-off times is identical whether ants bounce or pass through.
In pass-through mode each ant walks straight off; the last off simply started farthest.
The farthest start = max of 500 U(0,1) draws = E[max] = 500/501.

beats.interviewNote: E[max] = n/(n+1) < 1 for all finite n; it approaches 1 only asymptotically (n=9 → 0.9; n=99 → ~0.99). The 'E[max] must reach 1 eventually' trap fires here — values are bounded at 1.
beats.prompt: Drag n from 2 to 20. Watch E[max]=n/(n+1) and E[min]=1/(n+1) update. Does E[max] ever reach 1?

feedback.correct: E[max]=n/(n+1) creeps toward 1 but never reaches it — the values are bounded. E[min]=1/(n+1) sinks toward 0.
hints: At n=2: E[max]=2/3. At n=9: E[max]=9/10.
The gap to 1 is always 1/(n+1), however large n grows.

beats.prompt: Why does E[max] = n/(n+1)? Tap to expand the derivation.

interaction.title: Deriving E[max] = n/(n+1)
interaction.body:
**Why E[max] = n/(n+1):** The max of n uniform draws ≤ x iff every draw ≤ x, so F_max(x) = x^n.

Using E[X] = ∫₀¹ P(X > t) dt for X ∈ [0,1]:

    E[max] = ∫₀¹ (1 − x^n) dx = [x − x^(n+1)/(n+1)]₀¹ = 1 − 1/(n+1) = n/(n+1)

By symmetry (replace each draw x with 1−x): F_min(x) = 1−(1−x)^n, giving **E[min] = 1/(n+1)**.

hints: F_max(x) = x^n because every draw must be ≤ x.
The survival-integral formula: E[X] = ∫₀¹ P(X > t) dt for bounded X.
Integrate: ∫₀¹ (1−x^n) dx = 1 − 1/(n+1) = n/(n+1).

beats.prompt: Four IID Uniform(0,1) draws. What is the expected value of the minimum?

feedback.correct: E[min]=1/(n+1)=1/5. The minimum sits one slot below 1/4 — the formula always places it below 1/n.
hints: The minimum sits a bit lower than 1/4. The clean formula gives E[min]=1/(n+1)=1/5.
Plug n=4 into E[min]=1/(n+1).

beats.interviewNote: Ants on a string: 'n ants at random positions, bouncing on collision — expected clearing time?' Relabeling: collision ≡ pass-through → last-off ant = ant with max starting position → E[T] = E[max of n U(0,1)] = n/(n+1). For n=500: 500/501 ≈ 1 min. E[max] → 1 as n→∞ (values bounded at 1), so more ants barely changes the answer — the chaos collapses to a single-line expectation.
beats.prompt: Mastery challenge: use the relabeling trick to find the expected time for all 500 ants to fall off. Type an exact fraction.

interaction.scenario: 500 ants stand at random spots on a 1-foot stick and march at 1 ft/min. Collisions look like bounces — but relabeling shows each bounce is identical to passing through. The last ant off is simply the one that started farthest from its exit. What is \(E[\text{time until all 500 have fallen off}]\)?

feedback.correct: Relabeling collisions as pass-throughs, the clearing time equals the maximum initial walking distance. Each ant's position is uniform on \([0,1]\), so: \[ E[T] = E\!\left[\max_{i=1}^{n} U_i\right] = \frac{n}{n+1} \] The density of the maximum is \( n\,x^{n-1} \), giving \( \int_0^1 x \cdot n\,x^{n-1}\,dx = \tfrac{n}{n+1} \). With \( n=500 \): \( \tfrac{500}{501} \approx 0.998 \) min — more ants barely change it, since \( E[\max] \) saturates toward 1.
hints: More ants don't mean more time. Relabeling gives the max: \(E = \tfrac{n}{n+1} = \tfrac{500}{501}\), about one minute.

beats.prompt: Tap each card to close the Expected Value arc.

feedback.correct: Four tools, one move: list→weight→add. Variance — the spread — is next.
hints: The single move: list outcomes, weight by probability, add.
Two superpowers: linearity (sums always add) + conditioning (average the averages).
Order-stat extremes: E[max]=n/(n+1), E[min]=1/(n+1). Up next: how spread varies.
